title: Intuit's Discounted Valuation Sparks Debate: Buy, Hold or Sell the Stock?
detail:
Intuit's Discounted Valuation Sparks Debate: Buy, Hold or Sell the Stock?
Rohit Vasant Fatarpekar
Tue, June 16, 2026 at 10:12 AM PDT 5 min read
INTU
ORCL
^GSPC
ORCL-PD
ADP
Explore stocks on Coinbase
Trading disclosure

Shares of Intuit, Inc. INTU are trading at a discount. Based on the forward 12-month Price-to-Sales (P/S) ratio, INTU trades at 3.27x, well below the Zacks Computer - Software industry average of 6.33x.

The stock also carries a lower valuation than several industry peers. For comparison, Oracle Corp. ORCL trades at a forward P/S multiple of 6.13x, while Automatic Data Processing, Inc. ADP trades at 3.87x, highlighting Intuit's relatively discounted valuation.

 

Zacks Investment Research


Image Source: Zacks Investment Research

 

This comes amid a 38.2% decline of Intuit’s shares over the past three months, compared with the industry’s 0.9% growth and the S&P 500 composite 13% rise. Its peers, Oracle and ADP, have increased 22% and 6.7%, respectively, during this period.

Nevertheless, the decline has raised questions about whether this is another temporary stumble or a sign of deeper trouble in the computer-software landscape.

 

Zacks Investment Research


Image Source: Zacks Investment Research

 

Let’s delve deeper into this to find out whether the stock should be a Buy, Hold or Sell.

Why Do INTU Shares Fall?

Intuit acknowledged weaker-than-expected performance in its DIY segment in the third quarter of fiscal 2026. Management citied pressure among the most price-sensitive DIY tax filers earning less than $50,000 annually and noted that the company 'lost on price' in this segment. To reaccelerate growth in this segment, Intuit plans to refine its offerings and pricing structure to better serve simple filers’ needs at the lower end of the market while leveraging its broader Consumer platform to generate revenue opportunities beyond tax preparation.

Intuit continues to invest in product development, artificial intelligence and go-to-market initiatives, which is likely to put some pressure on near term profitability. In the third quarter of fiscal 2026, total costs and expenses rose to $4.54 billion from $4.03 billion in the prior-year period. The company also announced a 17% workforce reduction and expects to incur about $300-$340 million in restructuring charges, largely to be recognized in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2026.

What’s in Favor of INTU Stock?

Intuit has evolved from a tax- and accounting-software vendor into a unified platform that integrates TurboTax, Credit Karma, QuickBooks, Mailchimp and Intuit Enterprise Suite, serving consumers, small businesses and mid-market companies. This shift is now being reflected in the company’s fundamentals. In the third quarter of fiscal 2026, the company reported revenue growth of 10.4% year over year. As a result, it has raised its full-year revenue-growth guidance to approximately 13% to 14%.

Story Continues

title: Has The AI Power Boom Pushed Bloom Energy Stock Too Far, Too Fast?
detail:
Has The AI Power Boom Pushed Bloom Energy Stock Too Far, Too Fast?
Trefis Team
Tue, June 16, 2026 at 9:54 AM PDT 4 min read
BE
ORCL
^GSPC
APD
PLUG
Explore stocks on Coinbase
Trading disclosure
Trefis: BE Stock Insights

The fuel-cell maker is benefiting from a significant AI-driven trend, but its long-term value will be determined by its performance in less favorable market conditions.

If you’re watching the market’s top performers, Bloom Energy (BE) is hard to miss. The stock has jumped 8.3% in the last five trading days, easily outpacing the S&P 500’s 2.0% gain. The reason is a gusher of good news: on its latest earnings call, management announced a landmark deal to supply up to 2.45 gigawatts of power for Oracle’s (ORCL) “Project Jupiter” AI factory and sharply raised its full-year revenue guidance.

A run like that triggers a powerful instinct in any investor: the urge to chase a winner. You see a stock taking off, fueled by the insatiable power demands of artificial intelligence, and the fear of missing out can be overwhelming.

But building wealth isn’t about catching every hot stock. The more important question is what owning Bloom Energy does to your entire portfolio. Are you adding a genuinely different source of returns, or are you just piling on a more volatile version of the same market risk you already have in your index funds? How it behaves over years, not days, is what truly matters.

A Different Engine, Not Just A Faster One

To figure out if a stock is diversifying your risk, we look at its long-term relationship with the broader market. Bloom Energy’s five-year correlation to the S&P 500 is 0.41. At 0.41, Bloom lives in a valuable middle ground.

This moderate correlation means that while the stock often shares the market’s general direction, a substantial part of its performance is driven by its own story. And that story has been potent. Over the past five years, Bloom has delivered an annualized return of 58.6%, far outpacing the S&P 500’s 13.9%. That combination is attractive: you get a high-return asset whose success is meaningfully independent of the market you already own. Rather than being a leveraged copy of the index, it provides a differentiated return stream.

The Price Of That Power Is Volatility

That unique performance profile comes with a kick. Bloom Energy tends to amplify the market’s daily moves significantly. Over the past year, on days the S&P 500 went up, the stock captured about 551% of that gain. But on days the market fell, it absorbed about 344% of the loss.

This isn’t a flaw; it’s a crucial characteristic. The stock offers a powerful, distinct engine for growth, but it’s not a smooth ride. This high volatility underscores that sizing your position is critical. It’s an asset to be handled with discipline, not impulse.

Can Bloom Build Fast Enough For The AI Boom?

Behind these numbers is a business at a pivotal moment. The demand for electricity to power AI data centers is exploding, and the traditional grid can’t keep up. Bloom’s on-site fuel-cell servers, which management calls the “go-to choice for on-site power,” are positioned to fill that gap. The company’s confidence is clear in its updated guidance, which now projects 80% year-over-year revenue growth at the midpoint.

The primary risk here isn’t a lack of demand, but the challenge of execution. Management asserts that today, they are “not order constrained and not capacity constrained” and have shifted to “adding capacity continuously.” Scaling a complex manufacturing and supply chain operation at that speed is a monumental task. As the CEO noted regarding complex manufacturing scaling, managing potential operational hurdles will be key to preserving project timelines and not damaging the company’s core value proposition: speed to power.

For an investor, this means Bloom Energy can play the role of a high-growth, partial diversifier in your portfolio. Its returns are partly its own, which is valuable. But its volatility demands respect. Instead of chasing a five-day run, consider sizing a position based on its long-term ability to add a different dimension of risk and reward. The one signal to watch isn’t the stock chart, but the company’s operational updates: its ability to deliver on these large new projects on schedule will determine if the business can truly power the AI revolution.

So, How Should You Hold A Stock Like Bloom Energy?

Owning a strong performer is one thing; holding it in a way that fits the rest of your portfolio is another. The job is to size each position to the return it adds and the volatility it carries, so a single hot name never comes to dominate the risk you are taking. The Trefis High Quality (HQ) Portfolio is built on exactly that discipline, pairing the upside of strong businesses with the stability of a 30-stock portfolio, re-balanced with intent, and a track record of outperforming the S&P 500, S&P Mid-cap, and Russell 2000. Building a portfolio around how assets actually behave together, rather than which one ran hardest last week, is how you grow wealth while smoothing the ride.

title: Increasing Investor Concerns Dragged Oracle (ORCL) Lower
detail:
Increasing Investor Concerns Dragged Oracle (ORCL) Lower
Soumya Eswaran
Tue, June 16, 2026 at 8:38 AM PDT 3 min read
ORCL
ORCL-PD
Trade Oracle on Coinbase
Trading disclosure

Columbia Threadneedle Investments, an investment management company, released its first-quarter 2026 investor letter for the “Columbia Global Technology Growth Fund”. A copy of the letter is available to download here. In Q1 2026, the Fund’s institutional Class shares fell –6.05%, outperforming the S&P Global 1200 Information Technology Index, which declined –6.57%. Positive performance was mainly due to security selection in semiconductor and AI infrastructure companies, along with an underweight position in software and IT services. Broad markets declined amid a reversal in market dynamics, with energy and commodities surging while growth and tech fell sharply. The letter highlighted that, despite geopolitical risks and uncertainty, the U.S. economy continues to show resilience. In addition, you can check the Fund’s top 5 holdings for its best picks for 2026.

In its first-quarter 2026 investor letter, Columbia Global Technology Growth Fund highlighted stocks like Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL). Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL) is a leading global provider of products and services that enable enterprise information technology environments across multiple industries. On June 15, 2026, Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL) closed at $192.64 per share. One-month return of Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL) was 4.87%, and its shares lost 8.59% over the past 52 weeks. Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL) has a market capitalization of $554.04 billion.

Columbia Global Technology Growth Fund stated the following regarding Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL) in its Q1 2026 investor letter:

"Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL) shares fell about 25% during the quarter, as the market further digested the scale and financial intensity of the company’s AI infrastructure ambitions. The company reported strong quarterly growth with evidence of enterprise demand, and announced plans to raise $50 billion in financing to fund data-center buildout, along with a sizable headcount reduction. Further weighing on sentiment was the increasingly complex web of financing relationships and customer concentration, with OpenAI’s infrastructure needs representing a significant portion of Oracle’s forward commitments. Investors are demanding clearer evidence that the massive capital deployment will generate adequate returns to meet bullish expectations."

Oracle’s (ORCL) Strategic Innovation Boosts its Dividend Appeal

Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL) ranks 40 on our list of 40 Most Popular Stocks Among Hedge Funds Heading Into 2026. According to our database, 115 hedge fund portfolios held Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL) at the end of the first quarter, up from 111 in the previous quarter. While we acknowledge the potential of Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL) as an investment, we believe certain AI stocks offer greater upside potential and carry less downside risk. If you're looking for an extremely undervalued AI stock that also stands to benefit significantly from Trump-era tariffs and the onshoring trend, see our free report on the best short-term AI stock.

title: Can CRM's Record Cash Generation Support Robust Shareholder Returns?
detail:
Can CRM's Record Cash Generation Support Robust Shareholder Returns?
Anirudha Bhagat
Tue, June 16, 2026 at 6:10 AM PDT 4 min read
CRM
ORCL
ORCL-PD
Explore stocks on Coinbase
Trading disclosure

Salesforce, Inc. CRM has become one of the strongest cash-generating companies in the software industry, giving it significant flexibility to reward shareholders while continuing to invest in growth initiatives. The company’s robust cash flow performance is increasingly becoming a key part of the investment story.

In the first quarter of fiscal 2027, Salesforce generated a record $6.7 billion in operating cash flow and $6.6 billion in free cash flow. This strong cash generation was supported by healthy demand for its cloud applications, expanding adoption of AI offerings such as Agentforce and continued focus on operational efficiency. Revenues increased 13% year over year to $11.13 billion during the quarter, demonstrating that the company is still growing while generating substantial cash.

Salesforce has been using this financial strength to aggressively return capital. During the first quarter, the company launched a massive $25 billion accelerated share repurchase program, representing half of its authorized $50 billion buyback plan. The repurchase reduced diluted share count by 10% year over year, helping boost earnings per share and increasing the ownership stake of existing investors.

Salesforce’s balance sheet also remains healthy. The company ended the quarter with nearly $11.8 billion in cash and marketable securities, providing ample liquidity to fund strategic investments, acquisitions and shareholder returns simultaneously. The company’s growing profitability further supports this strategy. First-quarter GAAP operating margin expanded 130 basis points to 21.1%, while non-GAAP operating margin improved 250 basis points to 34.8%. These improvements indicate that Salesforce is converting a larger portion of revenues into earnings and cash.

With strong cash flow, rising margins and a large buyback authorization in place, Salesforce appears well-positioned to continue delivering meaningful shareholder returns while pursuing long-term growth opportunities. The Zacks Consensus Estimate for CRM’s fiscal 2027 revenues is pegged at $46.09 billion, indicating a year-over-year increase of 11%.

How Do Salesforce’s Peers Compare on Shareholder Returns?

Two major Salesforce competitors that also generate strong cash flows and return capital to shareholders are Microsoft Corporation MSFT and Oracle Corporation ORCL.

Microsoft remains one of the strongest cash generators in the technology sector. In the first nine months of fiscal 2026, the company produced more than $127 billion in operating cash flow and $47 billion in free cash flow. This financial strength allows Microsoft to maintain a balanced capital return strategy through both dividends and share repurchases. During the first three quarters of fiscal 2026, the company returned more than $37 billion to shareholders through buybacks and dividends. Its expanding cloud business, led by Azure’s 40% revenue growth in the latest reported quarter, continues to support rising cash generation and shareholder-friendly actions.

title: STMicro Eyes $1.5 Billion Convertible Bonds After AI-Fueled Jump
detail:
STMicro Eyes $1.5 Billion Convertible Bonds After AI-Fueled Jump
Bre Bradham and Charles Capel
Tue, June 16, 2026 at 12:48 AM PDT 2 min read
STMPA.PA
BNP.PA
STM
ORCL
ORCL-PD
Explore stocks on Coinbase
Trading disclosure

(Bloomberg) -- STMicroelectronics NV is looking to raise $1.5 billion from selling debt that can be exchanged into equity after the chipmaker’s shares tripled in value so far this year.

Most Read from Bloomberg

Iran’s Deputy Foreign Minister Confirms Deal Reached With US

Studios in Microsoft’s Xbox Division Brace for Closures

Netanyahu Pays a Political Price for Trump’s Iran Deal

US and Iran Agree to Halt War, Restart Middle East Oil Shipments

US at Odds With Allies Over How Easy It Is to Reopen Hormuz

STMicro is offering the convertible bonds — debt that can be swapped for shares at a pre-agreed price — across two tranches, one due in 2031 and the other in 2033. The proceeds will be put toward the early redemption of $750 million zero-coupon notes maturing in 2027, with the remainder used for general corporate purposes, the company said in a statement.

STMicro shares fell about 3% as of 9:42 a.m. in Paris trading. The stock has surged almost 200% so far this year, making it the third-best performer in the Stoxx Europe 600 gauge, amid booming demand for data center chips.

The French-Italian company, which counts Tesla Inc., Apple Inc. and Amazon.com Inc.’s AWS among customers, has expanded beyond consumer electronics and automotive chips into higher growth areas including data centers. Last month, it said revenue from that business will reach $1 billion this year, fueled by demand for artificial intelligence infrastructure, up from a previous forecast of more than $500 million.

The recent rally also means that the company’s share price is well above the conversion price on the 2027 notes of $45.10. Refinancing that bond removes the risk of share dilution, as investors are more likely to want to swap their debt into shares.

The tranche of convertibles due 2031 will have a fixed coupon of between 0% and 0.5%, and the conversion premium will be set between 47.5% and 52.5% above the company’s reference share price. The notes due 2033 will have a fixed coupon of between 0.625% and 1.125% and the conversion premium will be set between 50% and 55%.

STMicro is among a swathe of AI-related companies selling convertible bonds in recent months as issuers look for the cheapest way to fund investments such as data centers. Last month, network equipment supplier Ciena Corp. proposed a $2 billion offering of convertible senior notes, while AI firms Nebius Group NV and Oracle Corp. have also both done large convertible bond deals.

BNP Paribas SA and JPMorgan Chase & Co. are helping to arrange STMicro’s offering.

Most Read from Bloomberg Businessweek

title: Why Are Oracle (ORCL) Shares Soaring Today
detail:
Why Are Oracle (ORCL) Shares Soaring Today
Why Are Oracle (ORCL) Shares Soaring Today
Anthony Lee
Mon, June 15, 2026 at 7:44 PM PDT 3 min read
ORCL
ORCL-PD
Trade Oracle on Coinbase
Trading disclosure
What Happened?

Shares of enterprise software giant Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) jumped 5.7% in the afternoon session after yields fell as the Trump administration announced a new peace deal that would lead to the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

Software companies are among the most sensitive to long-term interest rates because their valuations depend on earnings projected years ahead. The discount rate applied to those forward cash flows is derived from the risk-free rate, in practice, the 10-year Treasury yield. When that yield drops to 4.41%, its lowest since mid-May, valuations across the sector improve without a single new contract being signed.

More from Yahoo Scout

What is Oracle's $638 billion backlog significance?

How did Oracle's Q4 financial results perform?

What concerns investors about Oracle's massive capital spending?

Why did Oracle shares jump 5.7% today?

Beyond the rate mechanics, the macro improvement matters for enterprise software specifically: customers who had deferred purchasing and renewal decisions during the period of geopolitical uncertainty now face a more settled planning environment.

Is now the time to buy Oracle? Access our full analysis report here, it’s free.

What Is The Market Telling Us

Oracle’s shares are extremely volatile and have had 33 moves greater than 5% over the last year. In that context, today’s move indicates the market considers this news meaningful but not something that would fundamentally change its perception of the business.

The previous big move we wrote about was 4 days ago when the stock dropped 12.6% on the news that it reported results that beat on almost every metric but disclosed a capital spending commitment that alarmed investors focused on free cash flow and dilution.

Q4 revenue grew 21% to $19.18 billion, cloud infrastructure (OCI) surged 93% to $5.8 billion, and the remaining performance obligations backlog (contracted future revenue) rose 363% year-over-year to a record $638 billion. Non-GAAP EPS beat the $1.96 consensus. Q1 FY2027 guidance of 27–29% revenue growth and EPS of $1.72–$1.76 both topped analyst estimates. The problem is the cost of delivering the backlog.

Oracle spent $55.7 billion in capital expenditures in FY2026 (up 162% year-over-year, above its own $50 billion projection) generating negative free cash flow of $23.7 billion despite $32 billion in operating cash flow. It then disclosed FY2027 capex of $70 billion plus $20–$25 billion in component prepayments, for up to $95 billion total.

To fund it, Oracle is raising another $40 billion in debt and equity, on top of the $48 billion already raised in FY2026. Stifel put the market's concern plainly after the call: OCI growth came in line with expectations, but management's FY2027 profitability commentary implies gross margins will decline more than previously modelled. The $638 billion backlog is real and the demand is genuine, but investors are being asked to absorb deepening negative free cash flow and accelerating share dilution before that contracted revenue converts to earnings.

Story Continues

title: DA Davidson Boosts Oracle (ORCL) Price Target Following Q4 Report
detail:
DA Davidson Boosts Oracle (ORCL) Price Target Following Q4 Report
Sultan Khalid

ORCL
^GSPC

Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL) is included among the 14 Best S&P 500 Stocks to Buy Now According to Analysts.

DA Davidson Boosts Oracle (ORCL) Price Target Following Q4 Report

Carol Gauthier/Shutterstock.com

Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL) is a global leader in AI, delivering the cloud infrastructure, data, and applications that organizations across the world trust to successfully achieve business outcomes at scale.

On June 11, DA Davidson boosted its price objective on Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL) from $200 to $225, while keeping a ‘Buy’ rating on the shares. The implied target represents an upside of over 22% from the current levels.

The move comes after Oracle exceeded top and bottom line estimates in its Q4 report on June 10. Notably, the company’s cloud infrastructure revenue jumped 93% YoY to $5.8 billion, while its remaining performance obligation, including revenue that hasn’t been recognized, grew by 353% YoY and hit $638 billion on May 31.

Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL) reaffirmed its prior revenue guidance of $90 billion for FY 2027. However, the company raised its adjusted earnings forecasts to $8.05 per share, marking a YoY growth of 18%. This is up from the projections of $8.01 per share in earnings and $88.9 billion in revenue by analysts. The cloud computing firm also remains confident in its long-term, financial targets, which include a plus 31% revenue CAGR and a plus 28% EPS CAGR through the fiscal year 2030.

While we acknowledge the potential of ORCL as an investment, we believe certain AI stocks offer greater upside potential and carry less downside risk. title: Intuit Stock's Real Growth Story Isn't The One You're Watching
detail:
Intuit Stock's Real Growth Story Isn't The One You're Watching
Trefis Team
Mon, June 15, 2026 at 6:07 PM PDT 3 min read
INTU
HRB
ADP
TRI
MSFT
Explore stocks on Coinbase
Trading disclosure
Trefis: INTU Stock Insights

The market is rightly focused on the company's struggles in do-it-yourself tax, but it might be missing a massive and much more lucrative business taking over from within.

If you’ve held Intuit (INTU) stock over the past year, you’re likely feeling the sting. The shares have fallen significantly, trading about 65% below their 52-week high, while the broader market has climbed. The culprit is a familiar story: the company’s core do-it-yourself tax business is sputtering at the low end.

Management admits it. They’re “constructively dissatisfied” with performance in the DIY segment and acknowledge they “lost on price” among the most price-sensitive filers. It’s a real problem, and it’s grabbing all the headlines. But what if the most important part of Intuit’s story is happening somewhere else entirely?

A Much Bigger Pond

While the market obsesses over the $5 billion DIY tax market, Intuit has been quietly and aggressively moving into a much larger arena: the $37 billion assisted tax category. This is the world where people pay an expert for the confidence that their taxes are done right, and it accounts for a staggering 88% of the total market.

This isn’t just a side hustle. It’s a strategic takeover. The company’s TurboTax Live offering, which connects filers with human experts, is rapidly becoming the main event. And the numbers show a business hitting its stride.

The Engine Ignites

According to the company, TurboTax Live revenue is expected to grow 36% this year, with customers growing 38%. That growth is so significant that the assisted offering will represent over half, 53% to be exact, of total TurboTax revenue this year. That’s a massive shift, up 11 points from just last year. While one part of the tax franchise is struggling with price, the other, much larger part is booming.

This is one of several “growth engines” management points to, including its mid-market and money portfolios, that are all growing “north of 30%.” The strategy appears to be a deliberate pivot toward higher-value, expert-driven services where price is less of an issue than trust and accuracy.

A Leaner Machine To Fuel The Fire

This strategic shift is happening alongside a major operational one. Intuit recently announced it is reducing its full-time workforce by 17%. While a tough decision, management frames it as a move to become a “faster, leaner and more focused company.” The goal is to sharpen the cost structure to scale its growth engines and deliver on its commitment to “durable long-term growth and margin expansion.” With operating margin already at a three-year peak, this move is designed to add fuel to the fire.

For investors, the narrative may be shifting. The question is no longer just about whether Intuit can fix its low-end DIY problem. The more critical question is whether the explosive growth in its assisted tax business is durable. The answer will determine if the company is simply managing a decline or engineering a powerful, and profitable, transformation. The key thing to watch is the growth rate of TurboTax Live customers. If that figure remains strong in the coming quarters, it will be the clearest sign that Intuit’s big bet is paying off.

Intuit is not alone in cutting costs to fund high-value growth. A similar dynamic of maximizing internal efficiency to boost profits while core customers face affordability hurdles is playing out across the market, a trend we analyze in Chewy’s Stock Is In The Doghouse. Its Profits Aren’t.

So, How Do You Position For The Upside?

Spotting a credible catalyst is one thing; capturing it without absorbing the wild, single-stock volatility that comes with it is another. The Trefis High Quality (HQ) Portfolio takes a different route: 30 quality names, sized and re-balanced with discipline, and a track record of outpacing the S&P 500, S&P Mid-cap, and Russell 2000. When it comes to protecting and growing wealth over the long term, a disciplined portfolio approach tends to pay better than concentrated single-stock bets.
